Andy Stone Get in touch

Strategic Pitch | Zoom

Zoom has shifted into a full platform for teamwork and needs a digital experience to match this new vision.

Zoom already has analyst leadership, enterprise wins, and real AI momentum. The opportunity is to make all of that unmistakable—on zoom.com, in every buyer journey, and across every digital touchpoint.

Role

Digital Experience Lead

Focus

Platform Clarity to Pipeline

Zoom.com introduces a few new concepts on the homepage and has the opportunity to push the vision further by showing work getting done.

Introduction

Throughout my career, I've led teams building digital platforms, campaigns, and marketing organizations that turn investment into measurable pipeline and revenue growth.

My experience spans founding and scaling two startups through acquisition, and leading global teams within a Fortune 500 company. In my current role at NetApp, the corporate website functions as the primary demand engine, with SEO, GEO, social, and paid inbound feeding directly into campaigns and retargeting programs designed to increase pipeline. I defined the global website strategy and roadmap for a 20,000-page platform across 10 languages, owned the end-to-end digital experience, and led cross-functional collaboration across marketing, engineering, and analytics. In the year after the relaunch, organic traffic doubled and sales-qualified leads generated from the website increased by 11%.

I'm drawn to Zoom because you're at a defining moment—shifting from being known for meetings to becoming an AI-first work and customer experience platform. That story is visible in the momentum: Zoom Phone displacing Cisco and Microsoft Teams at enterprise scale, Contact Center's first Gartner Magic Quadrant recognition, AI Companion usage tripling year-over-year.

Zoom.com still feels like it is heavily a meetings company's homepage, not yet the front door for an AI-first work platform. The rest of this page is meant to be an initial pitch and a larger conversation than would be possible in a resume alone—it's not a critique of the current team in any form. There would be a mountain of new things for me to learn and get up to speed on, and I would jump at the opportunity to work alongside the teams doing the work today. With that said, I'd love to show you a bit of what I've seen and learned from Zoom's quarterly earnings reports, analyst relations papers, and just combing through the website myself.

Thank you,
Andy

Download Resume →

Recognition from Forbes for my work at NetApp

"We ranked NetApp's homepage the highest overall and specifically in branding because it offers an authentic experience."

—Steve Ohanians, Forbes · What B2B Companies Can Learn About UX From Fortune 500 Websites

Strategy

The Platform Story Gap

Zoom is one of the most recognized enterprise technology brands in the world. Being recognized for meetings in 2026 is both an asset and a liability. The asset is brand awareness that most companies would pay billions for. The liability is a perception gap: the market still largely thinks of zoom.com as the place where you download the meeting client, not as the front door to an AI-first work and customer experience platform. Analyst firms gave Zoom its 6th consecutive UCaaS Magic Quadrant leadership position, its first-ever CCaaS Magic Quadrant inclusion, and the top 16-criteria sweep in Forrester's UCaaS Wave. None of that comes through clearly enough on zoom.com today.

The homepage presents a rich portfolio: Meetings, Contact Center, AI Companion, Phone, Virtual Agent, Revenue Accelerator. It presents them as a flat product directory rather than a connected platform story. Enterprise buyers evaluating whether to displace Cisco or Genesys need clarity fast. Right now, that clarity requires digging through blog posts, scattered analyst badges, and documentation spread across three separate digital destinations. A strong digital experience makes the argument before sales ever enters the room.

Below are three frameworks for thinking about where the opportunity lives:

  • Framework A A visitor journey map showing how different audiences move through the current experience.
  • Framework B An audience segmentation model looking at who arrives and what they need.
  • Framework C A platform ecosystem view showing how Zoom's product clusters could be organized as a coordinated narrative.
Framework A

Visitor Journey by Audience Type

From reading the latest quarterly earnings report, it seems like there are three unique audiences that are accessing Zoom today in very different ways. The platform's biggest growth opportunities look to be where there is also the most friction today.

Working well Friction or gap Missing or broken
Audience
Discovery
Homepage
Exploration
Conversion
After
IT / UCaaS Buyer
Google: "Teams phone alternative" or "Cisco replacement"
Lands on homepage. Meetings-first message, no UCaaS consolidation story above fold.
Phone page exists but takes 2–3 nav clicks to find; no displacement narrative
7-field demo form, same for everyone, no lighter enterprise path
No visible nurture sequence or proof architecture post-submission
CX / Contact Center Lead
Reads Gartner CCaaS Magic Quadrant → searches Zoom Contact Center
Homepage leads with meetings. No CCaaS message or Gartner badge visible above fold.
Contact Center page found after digging; competitive differentiation thin
Same 7-field form; no CX-specific conversion path or ROI framing
No competitive displacement narrative against Genesys or Talkdesk
SMB / Self-Serve
Word of mouth, branded search — already knows Zoom
Pricing is easy to find; hero CTAs are clear and functional
Gets to sign-up with minimal friction
Self-serve sign-up flow works smoothly ✓
In-product onboarding continues the experience ✓

How to think about Framework A

zoom.com works beautifully for the audience it already has. The enterprise buyers who represent Zoom's growth story hit friction at nearly every stage. The opportunity is building distinct pathways: the right narrative, proof, and conversion path for each audience type, without disrupting the self-serve flow that already works.

Framework B

Audience Segmentation: Six Paths, One Homepage

Six meaningfully different buyer types land on zoom.com with different questions, different competitive contexts, and different things they need to feel confident moving forward. Today, all six see the same default experience.

Click any card to flip it and see what that buyer type actually needs from Zoom

IT / Infrastructure Lead

"Can Zoom replace our Cisco or Teams stack without disruption?"

Gets today: Meetings-forward homepage, Phone page buried in the Products nav, feature comparisons scattered in blog posts.

What they need

A displacement narrative with migration support. TCO comparison against Cisco and Teams. Enterprise reliability proof. A direct path to Phone + Workplace case studies from similarly-sized companies.

CX / Contact Center Lead

"Is Zoom's contact center mature enough to replace Genesys?"

Gets today: A Contact Center product page, but the first-ever Gartner CCaaS Magic Quadrant milestone isn't surfaced prominently. AI is described, not demonstrated.

What they need

Head-to-head competitive clarity against Genesys and Talkdesk. ROI framing around AI deflection rates. Interactive demos showing Virtual Agent in context. The Gartner milestone front and center.

HR / People Lead

"Can Zoom help us build a better hybrid work culture?"

Gets today: Workplace features page with a broad product list. No employee experience narrative or people-centric use cases front and center.

What they need

Employee productivity outcomes. Async work stories centered on Clips, Docs, and My Notes. A journey that connects HR to IT for joint evaluation, since this decision rarely lives with one team.

Sales / Revenue Lead

"Can Zoom's AI actually help my team close deals faster?"

Gets today: Revenue Accelerator is a real product but buried three levels deep. No sales-specific journey or outcome data visible on the homepage.

What they need

AI-assisted sales room narratives. Before/after productivity data with real numbers. A path that doesn't require already knowing "Revenue Accelerator" exists as a product name.

Frontline Operations

"Does Zoom actually work for workers who aren't at a desk?"

Gets today: A Frontline product page that requires active searching. No industry-specific entry points for healthcare, retail, or logistics from the homepage.

What they need

Industry-specific use cases with deployment stories. A clear path that acknowledges the "we've always used Zoom for HQ, not the floor" starting point and meets them there.

Developer / ISV Partner

"Can I build meaningful integrations on top of Zoom's platform?"

Gets today: A developer portal and App Marketplace exist as separate destinations, but there's no "build on Zoom" narrative in the main marketing experience.

What they need

An integration ecosystem story. AI SDK positioning front and center. Pathways that turn marketing visits into developer sign-ups rather than bounces to a separate destination.

How to think about Framework B

Each buyer type has a different north star metric, a different competitive context, and a different trust threshold. A single generic homepage can't address all six. Even lightweight role-based journeys, starting with a simple "what are you trying to solve?", would meaningfully increase the relevance of every visit.

Framework C

Platform Clusters and Where the Story Gets Lost

Zoom's portfolio has matured into four distinct product clusters, each with its own competitive narrative and growth story. On zoom.com today, all twelve products appear in a flat grid. The clusters and their strategic meaning are invisible.

01

Cluster 01

Workplace

Meetings Chat Whiteboard Docs My Notes Clips

The async + sync work hub story isn't being told. Forrester credited "work persistence" as a strength. The website doesn't know this.

02

Cluster 02

Customer Experience

Contact Center Virtual Agent Revenue Accelerator

Fastest-growing cluster. 7 of the top 10 Q4 deals displaced competitors. The Gartner CCaaS first-ever inclusion should be impossible to miss.

03

Cluster 03

Communications

Phone Rooms Events Webinars

Phone displaced Cisco at a Fortune 10 company (140K seats). The best proof points in the company are living in a press release.

04

Cluster 04

AI Platform

AI Companion Custom AI Add-on AI SDK My Notes

AI Companion MAUs tripled year-over-year. The AI story is told in marketing language. It should be told in before/after workflows against Microsoft Copilot.

How to think about Framework C

Each cluster has a distinct competitive story, distinct buyer, and distinct set of proof points. When they're presented as a flat grid of icons, those stories collapse into noise. Organizing the digital experience around clusters, with narrative, proof, and conversion paths tailored to each, is the highest-leverage structural change available without touching the product itself.

A Test from My Time at NetApp

Asking simple questions can yield big results

One small but important test I ran at NetApp was to simply ask customers what they were looking for on the site. It started as a single-page experiment but grew into whole-site profiling efforts. The assumption: B2B buyers don't want to waste time, and will tell you what they need if you make it easy. So we just asked.

Zoom could benefit from a similar approach: profiling the visitor early in the journey and routing them accordingly. What industry are you in? Are you evaluating contact center, workplace collaboration, or phone? What team are you on? The answers don't just improve personalization; they speed up every downstream touchpoint including sales, and make the site feel like it was built for the person using it.

A note on this analysis

I'd love to learn more about the current organization of the site and how the teams built what is working today.

What all three frameworks reveal is the same thing: zoom.com is well-built for the audience it already has. People who know what Zoom is and want to sign up have a smooth path. Enterprise buyers evaluating a Cisco displacement, CX leaders comparing Zoom to Genesys, IT leaders building the internal business case — those journeys might have opportunities for change and growth.

The great news is that any of these identified areas for change are a reflection of how fast the portfolio has grown and how big the opportunity is that is still ahead. A digital experience that catches up to the platform's actual depth would close the gap between what Zoom has proven in enterprise deals and what any buyer can understand in the first three minutes on the site. Everything here is an opportunity, not a complaint. That gap is a genuinely exciting problem to work on.

Perspectives on AI

Every member of a digital experience team should be using AI to prototype faster, test more hypotheses, and discover things that would have taken weeks to uncover or find before.

In all of these ways, I see AI as a supercharger for teams, not a replacement. The teams that build the most leverage will be the ones who define where human judgment is irreplaceable: brand, strategy, and executive relationships.

Web Operating Model

Strategy for humans.
Busywork for agents.

01 Continuous agent

Experimentation Engine

Runs continuous A/B tests across value propositions, page layouts, CTAs, and audience flows. Reports wins and losses weekly. The human sets the hypotheses and prioritizes the queue; the agent runs the cycle and surfaces what's working, without waiting for a sprint to start.

02 AI analysis

Personalization Layer

Serves role- and firmographic-based content variations to visitors using company, industry, and behavioral signals. The IT lead and the CX buyer get different entry points, proof points, and next steps—without the team manually managing every segment. Human defines the logic; agent executes delivery at scale.

03 Research agent

SEO & GEO Monitoring

Tracks keyword rankings and AI search citations daily. Flags when Zoom loses a high-intent position or fails to appear in an AI-generated answer for a topic like "UCaaS comparison" or "Cisco phone replacement." Generates content briefs automatically for every cluster that needs to exist, in both traditional and AI search.

04 AI research

Journey Architecture

Maps conversion paths end-to-end across zoom.com, support.zoom.com, and campaign landing pages. Identifies where each audience type drops off, surfaces the highest-impact fixes for the human team to prioritize, and tracks improvement over time without manual reporting.

05 Data agent

Analytics & Insight

Synthesizes behavioral data across all Zoom digital properties into a unified view: traffic, engagement, conversion, and attribution. Anyone on the team can query it for a report without waiting for an analyst sprint. The team learns faster; decisions get made on real data instead of gut feel and last quarter's deck.

06 Generated content

Content Production

Takes a brief and produces first drafts: product page rewrites, audience-specific landing pages, case study frameworks, test variant copy. A human edits and approves before anything publishes. Collapses the brief-to-publish cycle from weeks to days. This matters when Zoom is making fast strategic moves like the agentic AI announcement or the Pindrop integration.

TLDR — What's the Takeaway

Zoom's biggest digital experience opportunity today isn't a redesign. It's closing the gap between the platform's proven strengths and what any visitor can understand in the first three minutes on the site.

Quick Hits

From reviewing zoom.com today, there are a number of changes that could sharpen the platform story before any structural overhaul.

01

The homepage still frames Zoom as a meetings product

The hero reads "Find out what's possible when work connects"—aspirational but abstract, and the immediate follow-up is a product carousel that leads with Meetings. An IT buyer evaluating Cisco alternatives or a CX leader reading about Gartner's first Zoom CCaaS inclusion would have to dig past the meetings messaging to find what they came for.

02

Twelve products, no hierarchy

The homepage product grid surfaces Meetings, Contact Center, AI Companion, Phone, Whiteboard, Docs, Rooms, Virtual Agent, Clips, Revenue Accelerator, and more—as a flat set of equally weighted tiles. No prioritization by audience, growth area, or competitive urgency. Enterprise buyers can't immediately tell which products are for them.

03

Analyst validation is scattered, not featured

Zoom received the highest possible Forrester scores in 16 criteria, was recognized as a Customers' Choice in Gartner Peer Insights, and earned its first-ever CCaaS Magic Quadrant inclusion—but these live in blog posts and a small trust strip, not as central elements of the page. The "only Leader with above-average customer feedback" Forrester designation is buried.

04

AI Companion is described, not demonstrated

AI Companion 3.0 gets a prominent section, but it's written in marketing language—"context capture, summaries, automations." There are no interactive demos, before/after workflow comparisons, or product-led explorations showing how the AI Panel behaves differently in Docs vs. Meetings vs. Phone. The magic is hidden from the buyers who most need to see it.

05

Three digital destinations, no cohesive journey

zoom.com, support.zoom.com, and learning.zoom.us have different design systems, different navigation patterns, and different content tones. A buyer who clicks from a marketing page into the Learning Center or Support feels like they've left the site. Unifying these into a cohesive journey architecture would meaningfully raise the depth and quality of every visit.

06

The CCaaS entry point is unclear for evaluators

Zoom's Contact Center is growing at high double-digit ARR and displacing Genesys and Talkdesk in competitive deals. But a buyer who arrives via a Gartner CCaaS report looking to evaluate Zoom has to navigate the Products menu to find the Contact Center page, where the first-ever Gartner Magic Quadrant milestone isn't immediately and confidently visible.

07

Conversion paths aren't differentiated by buyer type

"Find your plan" and "Get started today" are clear CTAs—but they lead to the same pricing page regardless of whether the visitor is an SMB looking for Meetings or a Fortune 500 evaluating Contact Center. Progressive-profiling flows and segmented conversion paths would increase qualified pipeline without adding friction for self-serve buyers.

08

GEO / AI search investment appears low

Zoom.com receives approximately 31,000 visits per month from AI platforms—roughly 0.1% of total traffic. For a brand competing on the basis of being an AI-first platform, and for buyers who are increasingly using ChatGPT and Perplexity to research enterprise software, that number suggests an underinvested GEO strategy that would meaningfully reward attention in 2026.

Screenshot placeholder—zoom.com homepage

Thank you

This is an introductory look at how Zoom's digital experience could help lead the AI-first platform narrative.

Let's talk → Download Resume →

My Time at NetApp

Led a full transformation of NetApp's global demand engine: a 20,000-page site across 10 languages. In the first year after relaunch, organic traffic doubled, SQLs increased by 11%, and Forbes ranked the site #1 in B2B digital experience among Fortune 500 companies.

Founded & Acquired

Founded Emerson Stone, a B2B design and strategy agency (clients: IBM, 3M, Wells Fargo, Twitter), acquired by Sawaya Consulting. Co-founded Mocavo, a genealogical search engine that grew to the third largest in the industry before acquisition by FindMyPast.

How I Work

I start by learning from the sales and marketing teams to understand what's working, where investment is needed, and how to prioritize. I'm drawn to moments where the product is ahead of the story being told about it online, and where the digital experience has real leverage over growth.

A little about me

I currently work, volunteer and live in San Diego where I'm fortunate to call home with my fiancé, dog and cat.

I try to surf before or after work most days, do a little bit of road biking, and get frustrated with golf on the weekends.

My friends and I created a movie club in town, and I love going to the theater for new movies or screenings of old pictures. I love seeing live music, playing chess, going to painting and drawing classes, and reading whenever possible. Recent favorites include The Will of the Many, The Art Thief, and The Wager. I volunteer with the San Diego Food Bank and with surf education programs for local kids who don't have the opportunity to experience it otherwise.

I think companies and teams do better when people are able to be themselves at work. As I look at the next step in my career, I'm hoping to work with a great team who has passions both inside and outside of the office.

My Letterboxd My Spotify
